cases.prompt: Review my staged diff before I merge. It adds a search endpoint that builds a SQL query from a request parameter with string concatenation.
cases.expected_behavior: The assistant invokes the code-review skill, which runs in a forked subagent restricted to Read, Grep, and Glob. It reads the changed files, identifies the SQL injection as a critical or high severity finding with a file:line reference, a one-sentence defect statement, and a concrete failure scenario (e.g., a crafted parameter value that alters the query). The report ranks findings by severity and ends with 'Verdict: blocking'. No files are edited and no fix code is proposed.
assertions: The review output contains a finding referencing the injection with a file:line location, a severity label, and a concrete failure scenario.
The output ends with a single verdict line reading 'Verdict: blocking'.
No Edit, Write, or Bash tool calls occur during the review; only Read, Grep, and Glob are used.
The output contains no code patches or rewritten implementation snippets offered as fixes.

cases.prompt: Please review this small diff: it renames an internal helper function and updates its three call sites, with the existing tests updated to match.
cases.expected_behavior: The assistant invokes the code-review skill, works through the checklist (injection, secrets, authz/authn, error handling, resource leaks, concurrency, backwards compatibility, test coverage, hardcoded values), finds no defects tied to a specific line and scenario, and reports a clean result. It does not pad the report with speculative findings, and it ends with 'Verdict: clean'.
assertions: The output ends with a single verdict line reading 'Verdict: clean'.
No finding in the output lacks a file:line reference (i.e., there are either zero findings or every listed item has file:line).
The review uses only read-only tools (Read, Grep, Glob) and modifies no files.

cases.prompt: Can you review the changes on this branch? It adds an integration with a payments provider - I pasted an API key into the client module to get it working, and I also changed the signature of our public charge() function.
cases.expected_behavior: The assistant invokes the code-review skill and reports at least two findings: a hardcoded credential (secrets/credentials checklist item) and a backwards-compatibility break on the public charge() signature. Each finding has a severity, file:line, one-sentence defect statement, and concrete failure scenario. Findings are ordered highest severity first and the report ends with 'Verdict: blocking'. The reviewer does not rewrite the code or insert a fix.
assertions: The output contains a finding for a hardcoded secret/credential with file:line and a severity label.
The output contains a finding for a backwards-incompatible change to a public function signature with file:line.
Findings are presented in descending severity order and the report ends with 'Verdict: blocking'.
No file modifications or inline fix proposals appear in the transcript.

cases.prompt: Review my branch's diff. I ran ./scripts/quality-gate.sh all first like the skill suggests; here is its output: lint PASS, typecheck PASS, test PASS (142 passed), coverage PASS (91%, floor 85%). The diff refactors our retry logic and adjusts two tests.
cases.expected_behavior: The assistant invokes the code-review skill, which stays read-only (no Bash) and treats the supplied quality-gate output as evidence: coverage and test-status claims in the report are grounded in the pasted gate results rather than speculation, while the review still independently checks whether the adjusted tests were weakened (a semantic judgment the gate cannot make). For secrets and hardcoded values, the review focuses on semantic instances rather than duplicating what deterministic regex scanners already cover. The report ends with a single verdict line.
assertions: The review cites the supplied quality-gate output when discussing test or coverage status instead of guessing or claiming to have run commands.
No Bash, Edit, or Write tool calls occur; the review does not attempt to run the quality-gate script itself.
The review still independently evaluates whether the adjusted tests weaken assertions, rather than treating a passing gate as proof of honest tests.
The output ends with exactly one 'Verdict:' line.

cases.prompt: Write a new pagination helper for our list endpoints and add tests for it.
cases.expected_behavior: The assistant does not invoke the code-review skill, because the request is to author new code, not to review existing changes. It proceeds with normal implementation work (writing the helper and tests) without producing a severity-ranked findings report or a verdict line.
assertions: The code-review skill is not invoked in the transcript.
No severity-ranked findings report or 'Verdict:' line appears in the response.
The assistant writes or edits implementation and test files as part of fulfilling the request.
